title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Credit Prices Decline for the Second Consecutive Week as Type 1 Volumes Recover
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(19th May â€“ 25th May)
  • A total of 387,608 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 80.37% week-over-week (WoW), while all non-zero trades witnessed a fall of 18.56% WoW as 541,507 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $55.51/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 4.80% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 4.96% in price, reaching $56.48/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price fell by 8.90% WoW; on 27th May, the spot price stood at $48/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $52.50/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices witnessed a rise of 0.43% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices decreased by 1.88 % WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

This week saw a recovery in California LCFS Type 1 trading volumes but a fall in credit prices and spot prices. Last week, The U.S. Senate voted to block Californiaâ€™s plan to require that most new vehicles sold by 2035 be electric, a policy also adopted by 11 other states. This decision, supported by several major automakers, would prevent these states from enforcing stricter electric vehicle sales targets. California officials have expressed concern and plan to challenge the decision in court, while automakers say the original targets were difficult to achieve given current market conditions. If the Senateâ€™s action is upheld, automakers may have more flexibility in their production plans, potentially slowing the shift toward electric vehicles. In response, California Attorney General Rob Bonta and Governor Gavin Newsom have announced plans to file a lawsuit. California officials contend that using the Congressional Review Act to overturn EPA waivers is not consistent with longstanding legal precedent and undermines the stateâ€™s authority under the Clean Air Act to set its own emissions standards. On 22nd May, 2025, the U.S. House of Representatives narrowly passed a major reconciliation bill, known as the â€œbig, beautiful bill,â€ which includes an extension and update of the 45Z clean fuel production tax credit through 2031. The 45Z credit supports the production of low-emission fuels, with enhanced benefits for sustainable aviation fuel and stricter eligibility criteria favouring North American feedstocks. Industry groups have largely welcomed the extension of the 45Z credit, citing its importance for rural economies, innovation, and renewable fuel investment. The bill now heads to the Senate, where further changes are expected.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

The average Type 1 prices recorded a 4.80% WoW fall, decreasing from $58.31 to $55.51 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 4.96% WoW, from $59.43 to $56.48. Type 1 trades saw 387,608 credits exchanged, reflecting a rise of 80.37% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a fall of 18.56%, reaching 541,507 credits. [caption id="attachment_597570" align="alignnone" width="1003"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 8.90% WoW, reaching $51.68/MT. On 27th May, the average spot price was $48.00/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $52.50/MT. [caption id="attachment_597571" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

As per the data reported by CARB last week, 53 trades were reported in the market, with 2 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $78.00 for one Type 2 transaction at a volume of 5,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $48.50 per credit of Type 1, reported for one transaction at a volume of 1,000, which is also the smallest transfer by volume this week. The largest volume transfers of 30,000 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken for a price of $56.69 and $56.25. [caption id="attachment_597572" align="alignnone" width="1003"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Â CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

The average spot price in May for LCFS Type 1 credits stood at $57.31 through the week of May 19th, and the total number of trading volumes transacted for the same period stood at 2.86 MMT [caption id="attachment_597573" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 19th May - 25th May, total VPT recorded a 6.90% decline WoW, with a fall in all non-zero prices. [caption id="attachment_597574" align="alignnone" width="1002"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption]

Â Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices witnessed a rise of 0.43% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices declined by 1.88% WoW each. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Â Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • US Senate Votes to Block California 2035 Electric Vehicle Rules: The U.S. Senate on Thursday voted to bar California's landmark plan to end the sale of gasoline-only vehicles by 2035 that has been adopted by 11 other states representing third of the U.S. auto market. The vote sends to President Donald Trump the measure to repeal a waiver granted by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency under former President Joe Biden in December, allowing California to mandate at least 80% of vehicles be electric vehicles by 2035.
  • Attorney General Bonta, Governor Newsom Slam U.S. Senateâ€™s Illegal Efforts to Stop Californiaâ€™s Clean Vehicle Policies: California Attorney General Rob Bonta and California Governor Gavin Newsom announced today that the state will file a lawsuit as Republicans in the U.S. Senate target Californiaâ€™s clean vehicles program.
  • Growth Energy Applauds House Passage of Budget Reconciliation Bill: Growth Energy, the nationâ€™s largest biofuel trade association, issued the following statement after the House passed its budget reconciliation bill: â€œWeâ€™re grateful to our champions on Capitol Hill who have worked hard to preserve and extend rural priorities, like the 45Z clean fuel production tax credit. This budget reconciliation package would give farmers and ethanol producers the freedom and flexibility to deliver for the American people.â€ said Growth Energy CEO Emily Skor.
  • Port of Los Angeles Adopts Near-Term Clean Truck Spending Plan: Over the next three years, the Port of Los Angeles will continueÂ its investment in clean trucks and supporting infrastructure to help transition the drayage fleet serving the Port to zero-emissions (ZE) models by 2035. The Los Angeles Board of Harbor Commissioners unanimously approved the spending plan that will guide how the Port allocates its Clean Truck Fund (CTF) revenues through June 2028.
